title: practical english usage - Part 121
content:

Turn to page 26. (     .. . the page 26.) Control to Car 27: can you hear me?

numbering and labelling               Go through door A instructions                                            Open packet at other end.

notices, posters, etc picture captions                                    Mother and child Section B headings                                                 Introduction Chapter 2 MAN  KILLED  ON  MOUNTAIN newspaper headlines We   usually leave out articles in abbreviated styles (►   291).

20 abbreviated         styles the;   my,    your,          this,    that,           Section12 Determiners:       a/an   and           etc;       etc grammar •  144   this and that but not Put those down - they're dirty, (          Tell those to gonway. ) but not This costs more than that, (          Ttes-says-h&dired;) normally only refer to things.

But when they are used without nouns (‘ pronoun’  use), this/that/these/those this child that house people or things.

This/that/these/those can be used as determiners with nouns that refer to either 2 people     and   things Determiner and pronoun uses are both covered in this entry for convenience.

Look at this. That's terrible!

They  can also be used without nouns, as ‘ demonstrative pronouns’ .

Look at that butterfly.  Let me show you these patterns.

grammars and dictionaries.) adjectives, though they are sometimes called ’ demonstrative adjectives’  in older be used as determiners at the beginning of noun phrases. (They are not This/that/these/those are often called ’ demonstratives’  in grammars. They  can 1  determiners         or  pronouns 144 this    an  d  tha   t  __     ___________ For the older English form thy, ►    318.10.   For southern AmE   you all's, ►     174.8.

For structures like Do you mind my smoking?, ►    94.3.   For my own, your own, etc, ►    552.

’ Whose little girl is that?' ’ You mean the one who's making all that noise?' The dog's in a good mood. It's just had its breakfast.

’ who  has’ . Compare: possessives, but contractions (►   337): they mean ’ it is’  or ’ it has’ ; ’ who  is’  or The  possessives its and whose have no apostrophes. It's and who's are not 6 spelling:     its,   whose not asleep, (     ... the eyes-closed and-the-hands-in-the-poekets-...) He stood there, his eyes closed and his hands in his pockets, looking half not Mia broke her leg playing football, (     Mia broke the teg-...) not She's got a parrot on her shoulder, (     She's got a parrot on the shoulder) In other cases we  do not normally use articles instead of possessives (►   142.16).

The ball hit him  on the head. She's got a pain in the stomach.

people's bodies.

talking about blows, pains and other things that often happen to parts of prepositional phrases which  refer to the subject or object, mostly when we  are We   sometimes use articles instead of my, your, etc. This happens in common 5 articles      instead      of  possessives:        a  pain     in   the    head not The teacher told the children to open their books, (     .. . their book:) ‘ one each’ . (For details, ►     131.) After a plural possessive, we  do not normally use a singular noun in the sense of 4 distributive        use:  She    told     them      to   open      their      books,       etc grammar •  144   this and that Who is this?                                -*• Americans can also use this to ask about the hearer’ s identity.

Hello. This is Max. Is that Alex?

about the hearer’ s identity.

On the telephone, British people use this to identify themselves, and that to ask 6 on   the   telephone 1 don't like that new boyfriend of yours.

Now tell me about this new boyfriend of yours.

show dislike or rejection. Compare: We   sometimes use this/these to show acceptance or interest, and that/those to 5 acceptance        and   rejection OK. That's it. I'm leaving. It was nice knowing you.

'Anything else? 'No, that's all, thanks.' (in a shop) ... and that's how it happened.
